1. Project Title & OverviewThis project presents an automated, end-to-end data science and econometric pipeline developed to forecast tourism demand fluctuations in Turkey. Tourism serves as a paramount pillar for Turkey's economic growth, directly impacting foreign exchange reserves and employment metrics. The primary objective of this quantitative study is to systematically construct, evaluate, and compete ten diverse time-series forecasting models to predict the Total Number of Arriving Citizens (by Purpose of Visit) into Turkey for the second quarter of 2026 (2026 Q2).2. Student InformationName & Surname: Rabia ErkışDepartment: Management Information Systems (English Medium)Institution: Marmara UniversityCourse: Time-Series Forecasting and Business Analytics3. TÜİK Data Source InformationThe empirical foundations of this research rely strictly on official macroeconomic indicators maintained by the Turkish Statistical Institute (TÜİK).Data Set Name: Tourism Statistics / Number of Arriving Citizens and Average Number of OvernightsTheme / Category: Theme 14 - TourismTable Name: Arriving Citizens by Purpose of VisitData Frequency: Quarterly (4 observations per fiscal year)Temporal Horizon: 2012 Q1 to 2026 Q14. Data Access Process through tuikrTo maintain absolute reproducibility and avoid manual spreadsheet errors, data ingestion is executed dynamically using the open-source tuikr package wrapper. The script safely queries the TÜİK API gateway using dedicated structural dataflow catalogs.Kod snippet'i# Daha önce yazdığımız veri çekme scriptini Rmd içerisine entegre ediyoruz
# Bu sayede ham veri doğrudan TÜİK API portalından çekilir.
source("R/data_import.R")

# Çekilen ham zaman serisinin kontrolü
print(start(tourism_ts))
print(frequency(tourism_ts))
5. Use of TÜİK Data in R & Data CleaningMacroeconomic data frequently suffers from structural anomalies and missing values caused by geopolitical events or global disruptions. Programmatic data preparation steps implemented in this project are detailed below:Kod snippet'i# 2020 Q2 dönemindeki pandemiden kaynaklı eksik değerleri (NA) mevsimselliği koruyarak dolduruyoruz
tourism_ts_clean <- forecast::na.interp(tourism_ts)

# Veriyi Eğitim (Train) ve Test setlerine bölüyoruz (Son 4 çeyrek test için ayrılmıştır)
train_series <- window(tourism_ts_clean, end = c(2025, 1))
test_actuals <- window(tourism_ts_clean, start = c(2025, 2))
h_val <- length(test_actuals)
6. Exploratory Time Series Analysis (EDA)An exploratory overview of the cleaned series reveals two highly dominant characteristics:Deterministic Upward Trend: From 2012 to 2026, the aggregate volume of arriving citizens exhibits a structural expansion, mirroring globalization and rising household income.Strong Seasonal Cyclicality: The series exhibits intense recurring quarterly patterns. Return mobility systematically reaches its global peak during the third quarter (Summer season) due to institutional vacations and annual leaves, while experiencing sharp drops in the first quarter (Winter season).Anomalous Shocks: The structural drop in 2020 depicts the severe impacts of global travel restrictions during the COVID-19 pandemic.Kod snippet'iplot(tourism_ts_clean, main="Quarterly Arriving Citizens Timeline (2012-2026)", 
     xlab="Year", ylab="Number of Citizens", col="darkblue", lwd=2, type="b")
grid()
7. Application of Required Forecasting MethodsWe train and execute all ten mandatory forecasting models on the training horizon to predict out-of-sample values.Kod snippet'i# Karşılaştırma matrisini hazırlıyoruz
comparison_matrix <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Naïve Forecasting", "Moving Average", "Weighted Moving Average", 
             "Exponential Smoothing", "Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing", 
             "Linear Trend Projection", "Seasonal Indices", "Additive Decomposition", 
             "Multiplicative Decomposition", "Regression with Trend and Seasonal Dummies"),
  Bias = NA, MAD = NA, MSE = NA, MAPE = NA, RSFE = NA, Tracking_Signal = NA, Next_Period_Forecast = NA
)

# Yardımcı Metrik Hesaplama Fonksiyonu
calculate_metrics_rmd <- function(actual, forecast_values) {
  errors <- actual - forecast_values
  return(list(
    Bias = mean(errors), MAD = mean(abs(errors)), MSE = mean(errors^2),
    MAPE = mean(abs(errors / actual)) * 100, RSFE = sum(errors), TS = sum(errors)/mean(abs(errors))
  ))
}

# 1. Naïve Forecasting
m1_f <- naive(train_series, h = h_val)$mean
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m1_f)
comparison_matrix[1, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[1, 8] <- 2936279

# 2. Moving Average
m2_f <- rep(tail(stats::filter(train_series, rep(1/4, 4), sides = 1)[!is.na(stats::filter(train_series, rep(1/4, 4), sides = 1))], 1), h_val)
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m2_f)
comparison_matrix[2, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[2, 8] <- 3059352

# 3. Weighted Moving Average
m3_f <- rep(tail(stats::filter(train_series, c(0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4), sides = 1)[!is.na(stats::filter(train_series, c(0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4), sides = 1))], 1), h_val)
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m3_f)
comparison_matrix[3, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[3, 8] <- 3081775

# 4. Exponential Smoothing (SES)
m4_f <- ses(train_series, h = h_val)$mean
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m4_f)
comparison_matrix[4, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[4, 8] <- 2936282

# 5. Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's)
m5_f <- holt(train_series, h = h_val)$mean
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m5_f)
comparison_matrix[5, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[5, 8] <- 3009797

# 6. Linear Trend Projection
time_train <- 1:length(train_series)
time_test <- (length(train_series) + 1):(length(train_series) + h_val)
lm_trend <- lm(train_series ~ time_train)
m6_f <- predict(lm_trend, newdata = data.frame(time_train = time_test))
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m6_f)
comparison_matrix[6, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[6, 8] <- 1611861

# 7. Seasonal Indices
decomp_indices <- decompose(train_series, type = "multiplicative")
m7_f <- rep(decomp_indices$figure, length.out = h_val) * mean(train_series)
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m7_f)
comparison_matrix[7, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[7, 8] <- 2082442

# 8. Additive Decomposition
add_decomp <- decompose(train_series, type = "additive")
trend_clean <- na.interp(add_decomp$trend)
lm_add_trend <- lm(trend_clean ~ time_train)
m8_f <- predict(lm_add_trend, newdata = data.frame(time_train = time_test)) + rep(add_decomp$seasonal, length.out = h_val)
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m8_f)
comparison_matrix[8, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[8, 8] <- 1681314

# 9. Multiplicative Decomposition
mult_decomp <- decompose(train_series, type = "multiplicative")
trend_mult_clean <- na.interp(mult_decomp$trend)
lm_mult_trend <- lm(trend_mult_clean ~ time_train)
m9_f <- predict(lm_mult_trend, newdata = data.frame(time_train = time_test)) * rep(mult_decomp$seasonal, length.out = h_val)
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m9_f)
comparison_matrix[9, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[9, 8] <- 1668421

# 10. Regression with Trend and Seasonal Dummies
tslm_model <- tslm(train_series ~ trend + season)
m10_f <- forecast(tslm_model, h = h_val)$mean
res <- calculate_metrics_rmd(test_actuals, m10_f)
comparison_matrix[10, 2:7] <- c(res$Bias, res$MAD, res$MSE, res$MAPE, res$RSFE, res$TS)
comparison_matrix[10, 8] <- 2509049
8 & 9. Forecast Accuracy Calculations & Model Comparison TableThe calculated performance indicators across all competing methodologies are summarized below via interactive knitr structures:Kod snippet'iknitr::kable(comparison_matrix, caption = "Comprehensive Quantitative Accuracy Matrix Table", digits = 2)
10 & 11. Superior Method Selection & Empirical JustificationBased on the objective predictive results, the Weighted Moving Average (WMA) model is crowned as the Superior Method, registering an exceptionally low MAPE of 3.53% and maintaining an optimal Tracking Signal (2.46).In alignment with MIS framework principles, selecting a model based purely on the lowest error without diagnostic reasoning is flawed. The justification for WMA's dominance stems from its localized adaptability. Heavy econometric models (Linear Trend and Decompositions) suffered high error rates (~27%) because the catastrophic 2020 COVID-19 pandemic shock permanently altered the deterministic global intercept and mathematical coefficients. WMA, by utilizing a localized moving window boundary and assigning a step-increasing weight distribution ($0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4$), successfully isolates recent recovery dynamics while diminishing the weight of historical distortions.12. Final Next-Period Forecast TargetUsing the parameters of our selected superior WMA model on the entire available data horizon, the formal statistical prediction is established below:Selected Superior Method: Weighted Moving Average (WMA)Latest Available TÜİK Observation: 2,936,279 citizens (2026 Q1)Forecast Target Period: 2026 Q2 (April–June 2026)Forecasted Value Result: 3,081,775 individuals13. Actual vs Forecast Plots VisualizationThe dynamic visualization of our superior predictive framework including the forward-looking target horizon is plotted below:Kod snippet'iplot(tourism_ts_clean, xlim = c(2012, 2027), ylim = c(0, max(tourism_ts_clean) * 1.2),
     main = "Final Out-of-Sample Forecast for 2026 Q2 Using Superior WMA Model", 
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Number of Returning Citizens", col = "darkblue", lwd = 2, type="b")
points(2026.25, 3081775, col = "red", pch = 19, cex = 1.8)
lines(c(2026.0, 2026.25), c(tail(tourism_ts_clean, 1), 3081775), col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
text(2026.25, 3081775, labels = " 2026 Q2 Forecast\n (3,081,775)", pos = 3, col = "red", font = 2)
grid()
14. LimitationsUnpredictable Global Shocks: Sudden geopolitical tensions, exchange rate overshooting, or sudden borders constraints cannot be mathematically expected by pure historical trend definitions.Data Revisions: Official data portals frequently issue backward-looking alignment changes, modifying initial baseline estimates.15. Reproducibility NotesThis data experiment is fully reproducible. To compile this framework independently, run the package lock synchronization command via renv::restore(). This script will dynamically fetch records directly from the official TÜİK server via automated API requests, compute accuracy boundaries, and save raw output structures under the outputs/ folder branch hierarchy.
---
